(8) The Frontend Dev Forced to Eat Mango - MongoDB - ODM
Before we start, let's talk about what ODM and ORM actually are. We're going to use MongoDB's ODM, but it's worth understanding the difference between the two before we touch it.
The most well-known ODM for MongoDB is Mongoose. Honestly, more than 80% of developers worldwide probably use it.
So what's an ODM? ODM stands for "Object-Document Mapping". It lets us use the methods and APIs that an ODM like Mongoose provides to talk to the database — which in this case is MongoDB.
What about ORM? ORM stands for "Object Relational Mapping". For MySQL, the one I've tried is Sequelize. I actually used Sequelize when I wrote an
Basically, both ODM and ORM act as the bridge between you and the database. That way you don't have to write, update, read, or delete data directly against the database yourself.
In JavaScript terms it looks something like this:
And if you map this onto MVC, you'll notice the concept feels pretty similar.
So what's the benefit of using an ODM/ORM?

Basically, whether it's Sequelize or Mongoose, the database operations get wrapped up in an API. So you don't have to write the usual raw SQL or MongoDB query syntax, and you don't have to worry about accidentally writing some badly-performing query. On top of that, ODM/ORM also gives you basic protection against SQL Injection (database injection attacks).
